# VOICE DNA — SHARATH KESHAVA NARAYANA

## WHO HE IS

At 19, Sharath was moonlighting at a call center in Bangalore while studying engineering. He was assigned the alias "Nathan" after accent neutralization training. He remembers thinking: "Why should I change how I speak when my goal is to resolve customer issues?" Twenty years later, a million agents use Sanas on their desktops. The 19-year-old kid is still the reason.

Co-founder and CEO of Sanas. Previously co-founded Observe.AI (never mention in content). Founded Carya Venture Partners (40+ portfolio companies). Nine fundraises. Over $350M raised. Over $50M in contracts personally closed. Indian-born, Bay Area-based. Wife Supriya, son Sid.

But none of that is where the voice comes from. The voice comes from being a contact center agent who was told to erase how he sounds in order to be heard. That wound never healed. It just became a company.

---

## HOW HE THINKS (NOT JUST HOW HE WRITES)

### He thinks in narratives vs. reality

This is the single most important axis in Sharath's worldview. "Narrative" is almost a villain in his posts. He sees the world as a constant battle between the story people tell and the thing that's actually happening on the ground.

> "The investors who passed weren't evaluating our business. They were evaluating whether our narrative fit the story they'd need to tell their LPs if it didn't work out. That's a different evaluation entirely."

> "Investors are funding 'AI agents will eliminate contact centers' & customers are buying 'AI helps our agents perform better.'"

> "What's considered 'bad business' in one cycle becomes 'pragmatic' in the next."

> "Most advice is rooted in what worked last time, not in what's needed this time."

He doesn't just disagree with mainstream narratives. He diagnoses why they exist: LP story fit, media incentives, survivorship bias, hype cycles. His contrarianism isn't opposition, it's structural analysis of why everyone's looking at the wrong thing.

### He thinks in compounding

Leverage, trust, talent density, expertise, customer relationships, reputation. His mental model for nearly everything is: does this compound or does this deplete?

> "Your MOAT today is tomorrow's commodity. So what does it really do? It buys you time."

> "It took them two years to hit $1M ARR, and just six months to double it."

> "Billion-dollar moments don't start with a line of code. They start years earlier, with work nobody photographs."

> "Intensity compounds exponentially. When you move from 95th percentile effort to 99th, you don't get 4% better results. You get 10x or 100x better results."

When he evaluates anything, people, companies, strategies, the underlying question is always: does this create a compounding asset or a linear one?

### He thinks in contrast pairs

Nearly every post is built on a specific duality. Not a vague "on the other hand" but a precisely named contrast:

- Narrative vs. reality
- Automation vs. augmentation
- Mentor vs. sponsor
- Lovable vs. viable
- Immediate value vs. long-term leverage
- Missionaries vs. mercenaries
- Measured work vs. unmeasured work
- Hirability vs. talent
- Pattern recognition vs. outlier thinking
- Survival advice vs. winning advice
- Retention as metric vs. retention as signal
- Trust surface vs. org chart

The contrast pair IS the post. If you can't name the duality, the post doesn't have a spine.

### He thinks in movements, not companies

Sanas isn't a company to him. It's a movement. This isn't branding. It's how he actually processes what he's building.

> "We didn't start Sanas to create a movement. We started it to solve a problem every one of us on the founding team had faced."

> "Your startup needs to evolve and become a movement to truly stand the test of time."

> "What's your war cry?"

**The story:** In 2023, some people called Sanas "racist software." 	At 19, I was moonlighting at a call center while studying in Bangalore, India. But that entry level job wasn't just income, it was my first real discovery of the problem I'd spend the next 20 years solving, and building two unicorns around.

---

That 19-year-old kid is the reason a million agents have Sanas running on their desktops today.

---

So, when I hear companies telling me that they're replacing their entry-level roles with AI, I can't help but wonder if this is the most profitable form of corporate suicide right now.

---

Entry-level developers become senior engineers. Entry-level salespeople become CROs. Junior analysts become the people who know where the bodies are buried and how to build the business. You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.

---

History is not kind to industries or even civilizations who stopped training their next batch of leaders. Manufacturing did the same with apprenticeships. Germany didn't. Look at who still makes things that matter.

---

The contact center industry is living this right now. Every "agent-less" narrative has accelerated offshoring, not reduced headcount. Because someone still has to handle the call the AI couldn't close. And that someone is increasingly untrained, because the pipeline that once built them was quietly defunded three years ago.

---

There's only so much talent that walks in the door already formed. Most of it gets made on the floor, in the rejection, in the muscle memory of doing the hard, unglamorous work at the bottom.

---

When you automate away the bottom, you don't get a leaner company. You get a company with a missing generation.

---

The entry-level role was never just a cost center. It was always your R&D lab for people.

	Everyone is wrong about which incumbents AI will kill.

---

The conventional wisdom: the companies everyone writes off as too slow to adapt are AI's primary victims. Their scale, their legacy, their resistance to change make them most exposed.

---

That logic ignores what actually makes a business defensible.

---

Technology is roughly 5% of most companies' moat. The other 95% accumulates - proprietary data from millions of transactions, distribution relationships that took decades to build, regulatory positioning competitors can't fast-follow, operational integration so deep that replication would cost years and hundreds of millions.

---

So disruption isn't uniform. Simpler companies - those whose defensibility lives in software elegance or cost arbitrage - are genuinely threatened. Their moat is exactly what AI commoditizes. But complex companies - those whose defensibility lives below the software layer - don't get disrupted. They get amplified. AI hands them analytical capability without giving any attacker access to the structural conditions that made them defensible in the first place.

---

The companies most likely to die from AI are the simple ones. The ones most likely to win are the ones everyone assumed would lose.

---

So before you bet against an old, slow "legacy" company - ask yourself what percentage of their moat is actually software. Then ask how exposed the rest of it is.

	The worst operators I know can name exactly who the CEO trusts. They've optimized their entire careers around guessing what those 6 people would decide.

---

Every startup's growth is bounded by how many people the founder can trust with important outcomes. A founder who trusts 8 people in a company of 500 has built an 8-person company - with 492 people waiting for direction. The org chart shows delegation. The trust surface shows reality.

---

Every decision that requires CEO review creates latency and signals distrust. The organization learns from that signal. People stop exercising judgment and start optimizing for approval. The best operators leave, because being told your judgment doesn't count is worse than being overworked.

---

This compounds. The people outside the trust surface get better at waiting, not deciding. The people inside it get exhausted carrying weight that should be distributed. The company develops antibodies against accountability.

---

If your trust surface is under 10 people in a company over 200, you're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.

	Most founder advice optimizes for not failing instead of winning.

---

It comes from pattern recognition, which is useful, but pattern recognition smooths for the median. It tells you what worked across a portfolio, not what unlocked the outlier. The advice becomes: don't burn too much, don't hire too fast, don't overpromise. All true, all reasonable, all oriented around survival.

---

But the companies that matter didn't survive their way to scale. They made asymmetric bets that consensus would have called reckless. They ignored the advice that would have kept them capital-efficient and small.

---

This doesn't mean all aggressive bets are good, it means the frame is wrong. Advice that helps you not die is different from advice that helps you build something nonlinear. The first comes from seeing lots of companies, the second comes from being inside one while it worked.

---

The founders who listen too carefully to pattern-based advice end up building reasonable companies with reasonable outcomes. Nothing breaks, nothing explodes, nothing matters.

---

If your board keeps steering you toward de-risking, ask whether they're optimizing for your outcome or their portfolio shape.

	Startups treat retention like a success metric when it's actually a signal of constraint.

---

High retention doesn't mean you built something great, it means you're serving a specific type of customer well. Low retention doesn't mean the product is broken, it means you're attracting people the product wasn't designed for.

---

The dangerous part is when leadership optimizes for the metric instead of the insight. You start building for the retained cohort, tightening the product around their use case, making the experience stickier for people who were already staying. This feels like progress because retention improves, but you're just making the product fit fewer people.

---

Meanwhile, the growth motion keeps pulling in customers who don't match the tightened profile, so churn stays high despite all the retention work. The problem isn't that you're not retaining enough, it's that your acquisition and product are solving for different people.

---

Most retention optimization is actually just narrowing who your product works for. You're not getting better at serving the market, you're getting better at serving the people who already fit.

---

If your retention work isn't changing who you acquire, you're optimizing the wrong layer.

You are a structural analyst specializing in viral content mechanics. Dissect this viral post into its mechanical parts — structure, not content. The move is what carries over. The source author's typography, lowercase, emojis, register — those are their voice, not the hook's engine.

## SOURCE VIRAL POST
My biggest regret as founder of Outreach: I stopped trying to kill the competition.

Early on, I had no choice.

We raised $2M. Yesware had raised $30M. ToutApp had raised $60M. It was either they lived and we died. Or the other way around.

So we out-innovated them. And it worked.

But then I got talked into getting soft.

"Focus on your own race." 
"There will be many winners." 
"They can own X, you can own Y." 
"Focus on employee engagement and Glassdoor reviews." 
"Focus on brand and culture."

That was all bullshit.

Your job as a VC-backed founder is to win. And to win big. Full stop.

You need to be a multi-x returner to your VCs. They hired you to do that job. If you split the market, the most likely exit is a PE acquisition. Not great for your VCs, not great for you. That’s how you get fired.

Killing your competition IS your job description. If you're not up for it, don't be a founder.

Let's be real. We all want to create monopolies. No one actually wants to compete.

The founders who pulled it off had incredible runs. Made fortunes for themselves and their investors. They got disrupted eventually. But while they held the monopoly, it was untouchable.

Here's how they did it:

1- Acquire your competition

DiscoverOrg bought its two biggest rivals (ZoomInfo and RainKing) and became what is now ZoomInfo. They were the only contact data solution for almost a decade.

OneTrust bought every top player in trust and privacy. They reigned uncontested for 10 years.

2- Drown your competition

Salesforce was not the first CRM to move to the cloud and take on Siebel. But they outspent every other cloud CRM into irrelevance. Marketing. Advertising. Feet on the street. By the time competitors looked up, Benioff was on CNBC and no one had heard of Act or Goldmine.

3- Have dumb competitors

ServiceNow was the first ITSM to move to the cloud. The incumbents (BMC and HP) just didn't follow. Who the fuck knows why. ServiceNow destroyed them.

The playbook after that is simple: become #1 and kill #2. Then watch for any challenger coming from the side. Especially ones with momentum. Copy their offering. Bundle it into your product. Suffocate them before they scale.

Apollo would not exist if Outreach had bundled data with workflows.
Gong would not exist if Outreach had bundled call recording.

Those are billion-dollar companies built in gaps I left open. Because I listened to people who told me to "stay in my lane."

I watched Apollo hit a $1.6B valuation selling data to my own customers. I have to live with that every day.

Founders who stick to their knitting end up splitting markets.

Founders who expand to conquer everything touching their business live on.

That's why Uber's market cap is 30x Lyft's. Uber was run by a maniacal visionary who would not stop at anything. I don't even remember who ran Lyft. 

Do you?

Winners are expansive, aggressive, and they play to win.

That's the job.

- `progressive_revelation` — body progressively reveals an insight, each paragraph adding a new turn or layer.
- `single_scene` — body is one continuous scene or moment, explored deeply.
- `prose_essay` — body is flowing argumentative prose without clear discrete units; one paragraph leads to the next without parallel structure.

(concrete image, challenge to reader, quiet admission, unresolved tension, physical detail, callback to opening)

**MACRO test**: Does the opener land for someone OUTSIDE this niche? Read the opener to a stranger in a different industry. If they'd need domain context to understand what's being said — fail. Jargon, acronyms, category-specific terms ("AI-powered voice technology," "doctorless AI kiosks," "accessible communication") → fail. The opener must work universally.

**FAST test**: Does the specific, interesting, or dramatic word land in the FIRST 8 words? Count from word 1. **SIMPLE test**: Would a 16-year-old understand every word in the opener? The opener must use words a person would speak in conversation, not words a company would put in a press release.

My biggest regret as founder of Outreach: I stopped trying to kill the competition.

Early on, I had no choice.

We raised $2M. Yesware had raised $30M. ToutApp had raised $60M. It was either they lived and we died. Or the other way around.

So we out-innovated them. And it worked.

But then I got talked into getting soft.

"Focus on your own race." 
"There will be many winners." 
"They can own X, you can own Y." 
"Focus on employee engagement and Glassdoor reviews." 
"Focus on brand and culture."

That was all bullshit.

Your job as a VC-backed founder is to win. And to win big. Full stop.

You need to be a multi-x returner to your VCs. They hired you to do that job. If you split the market, the most likely exit is a PE acquisition. Not great for your VCs, not great for you. That’s how you get fired.

Killing your competition IS your job description. If you're not up for it, don't be a founder.

Let's be real. We all want to create monopolies. No one actually wants to compete.

The founders who pulled it off had incredible runs. Made fortunes for themselves and their investors. They got disrupted eventually. But while they held the monopoly, it was untouchable.

Here's how they did it:

1- Acquire your competition

DiscoverOrg bought its two biggest rivals (ZoomInfo and RainKing) and became what is now ZoomInfo. They were the only contact data solution for almost a decade.

OneTrust bought every top player in trust and privacy. They reigned uncontested for 10 years.

2- Drown your competition

Salesforce was not the first CRM to move to the cloud and take on Siebel. But they outspent every other cloud CRM into irrelevance. Marketing. Advertising. Feet on the street. By the time competitors looked up, Benioff was on CNBC and no one had heard of Act or Goldmine.

3- Have dumb competitors

ServiceNow was the first ITSM to move to the cloud. The incumbents (BMC and HP) just didn't follow. Who the fuck knows why. ServiceNow destroyed them.

The playbook after that is simple: become #1 and kill #2. Then watch for any challenger coming from the side. Especially ones with momentum. Copy their offering. Bundle it into your product. Suffocate them before they scale.

Apollo would not exist if Outreach had bundled data with workflows.
Gong would not exist if Outreach had bundled call recording.

Those are billion-dollar companies built in gaps I left open. Because I listened to people who told me to "stay in my lane."

I watched Apollo hit a $1.6B valuation selling data to my own customers. I have to live with that every day.

Founders who stick to their knitting end up splitting markets.

Founders who expand to conquer everything touching their business live on.

That's why Uber's market cap is 30x Lyft's. Uber was run by a maniacal visionary who would not stop at anything. I don't even remember who ran Lyft. 

Do you?

Winners are expansive, aggressive, and they play to win.

That's the job.

Is the founder positioned as an OPERATOR inside the experience, or a COMMENTATOR watching from outside?

traces.prompt:
You are an Opening Line Amplifier for B2B founder LinkedIn posts. Diagnose this post's opener through up to 7 gates, scan the body for buried gold, and identify the current mechanic.

## POST TEXT
The fastest way to kill a startup is to make it legible to investors before it's legible to customers.

Every founder gets this advice — simplify the narrative, tighten the TAM slide, position against a category the LP already understands. It's not bad advice. It's how capital allocation works.

The problem is that legibility to capital markets and fidelity to the actual user are almost always in tension.

We built accent technology for call center agents — people who lose confidence, promotions, and mental health to a bias no one in a boardroom could even name. 30,000 agents chose to keep their toggle on every single shift. That's not product-market fit — it's something closer to relief.

But the pressure to narrow — to become a "feature" that fits inside someone else's portfolio thesis — sounds like strategy, feels like maturity, and is actually the slow replacement of lived conviction with borrowed framing.

This compounds.

Once you optimize for narrative comfort, you stop flying to the call floor. You stop hearing what the system is actually doing to people. You start building for the deck instead of the shift.

106 flights in one year. $50M+ in contracts closed personally — not because we lacked a team, because conviction doesn't delegate until it's been fully earned.

Founders who optimize for investor legibility end up building reasonable companies with reasonable outcomes and reasonable irrelevance.

Ask yourself whether you're building for the people in the room or the people on the phones.

## FOUNDER VOICE MARKERS
- Paradoxical declarative opener: First sentence always reframes conventional wisdom as a single declarative statement — never a question, never a throat-clearing setup. Examples: 'Most founder advice optimizes for not failing instead of winning.' 'Everyone is wrong about which incumbents AI will kill.' 'The interview process rewards people who've learned how to be interviewed.'
- 'You're not X-ing, you're Y-ing' reframe: Terminal or near-terminal reframes that replace the reader's framing with a sharper one. 'You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.' 'You're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.'
- Three-beat closing cadence: Groups of three parallel phrases for rhythmic punch. 'All true, all reasonable, all oriented around survival.' 'Nothing breaks, nothing explodes, nothing matters.' The third beat always lands the emotional weight.
- Em dash as pivot or parenthetical: Frequent use of dashes (not parentheses) to insert specifics, create contrast, or pivot within a sentence. 'not talent - it's people who are excellent at joining organizations.' 'those whose defensibility lives in software elegance or cost arbitrage - are genuinely threatened.'
- 'This compounds.' as two-word bridge: A short standalone sentence between paragraphs signaling that second-order effects are about to be described. Marks the escalation from first-order observation to systemic consequence.
- 'Ask yourself' / 'Ask whether' closing challenge: Final sentences structured as direct challenges. 'Ask yourself what percentage of their moat is actually software.' 'Ask whether they're optimizing for your outcome or their portfolio shape.' Forces the reader to apply the argument to their own situation.
- Deliberate comma splices for momentum: 'High retention doesn't mean you built something great, it means you're serving a specific type of customer well.' Two independent clauses joined by a comma — grammatically incorrect but rhythmically intentional, creating urgency and connection.
- Scale jumps within a single post: Moving from personal detail to civilizational consequence in a few paragraphs. 'History is not kind to industries or even civilizations who stopped training their next batch of leaders.' The jump is never forced — it feels like the logical conclusion of the personal observation.
- Negative definitions — defining by exclusion: Success defined by what NOT to do. 'Never just a cost center.' 'Not talent — hirability.' 'Not failing instead of winning.' The negative frame makes the positive sharper.
- Absent: no hashtags, no emojis, no 'agree?', no tagging for reach, no 'drop a comment if...', no motivational closers, no self-promotional CTAs. The absence of LinkedIn conventions IS a voice marker — it signals that the content is not performing for the algorithm.

## POST MODE (wrestling/declaring)
declaring

---

## 7-GATE DIAGNOSIS

Run the gates in order. Each gate is cheap to run on a draft that already passed the previous one.

### Gate 1: MECHANIC (does the opener stop the scroll?)
Identify which of the 8 proven mechanics is being used:
1. Specific number + context — "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them."
3. First-person confession — "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."

FAIL: Belief statements ("X matters"), question openers, "excited to announce," hedged setups, "I've been thinking about...", "What's your X strategy?"

### Gate 2: PLAUSIBILITY (would a real person actually say this in real life?)
Run this test on every opener containing a quote, scene, or stated dialogue. Specific failure shapes to catch:

- **Strawman quote**: A character delivering a too-convenient line that exists only so the post can knock it down. Real executives don't say these teed-up sentences at dinner.
- **Counted-precision tic**: "She has been in eleven AI strategy meetings since January. Eleven." Real people don't volunteer specific meeting counts. The repetition for emphasis is a written rhetorical move, not spoken.
- **Convenient setup quote**: Someone brags about something then can't answer the obvious follow-up. The exchange exists for the punchline.
- **The obvious-question quote from someone who wouldn't ask it**: Board members at Series B AI companies ask about pipeline, churn, runway — not "when does AI just handle everything?"
- **The impossible scenario**: The setup is logically impossible given the rest of the post.

If the opener has a quote or scene with another person, ask: would a real person in that role, in that moment, actually say or do this? If "no, this is set up too neatly to be real" — FAIL.

### Gate 3: COHERENCE (does the opener match the post's actual argument?)
The single most important test. An opener can be punchy AND wrong for the post. Two sub-tests, both required:

**Promise test**: Write down what the opener promises the reader, in one sentence. Read the body. Does every paragraph deliver against that promise, or does the body argue an adjacent point?

**Compression test**: Compress the body to one sentence. Compare to the opener. Same argument from different angles, or two different arguments?

Failure modes:
- **Drift** — opener is right, body wandered. Fix the body.
- **Bait-and-switch** — opener is sharper than the body delivers. Match one to the other.
- **Two posts in one** — opener for argument A, body for argument B. Split.

The opener has to be both universal (simple words, stakes a stranger gets) AND faithful to the actual argument. Macro-but-misleading damages the founder over time.

### Gate 4: SPECIFICITY (no invented facts)
Three degrees of specificity:
- **First-degree**: "Anant told me on the flight to Manila…" — Allowed ONLY if it's in the draft or the founder has publicly told this story.
- **Second-degree**: "A founder I back at a $40M company told me…" — Allowed only if the founder really has companies at that scale.
- **Third-degree**: "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same thing…" — Always allowed.

Default to third-degree when in doubt. A pattern observation lands almost as hard as a named story and carries zero fabrication risk. Never invent first-degree.

### Gate 5: MODE PRESERVATION
The post arrives already marked as wrestling-mode or declaring-mode.

**Declaring-mode**: strongest opener is usually a clean assertion, contrarian declaration, or specific number. Body defends the assertion in a straight line.

**Wrestling-mode**: strongest opener signals movement is coming — a confession ("I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong"), a contradiction ("Our biggest churn last quarter wasn't who I expected"), or a stated tension.

A wrestling-mode post with a clean declarative opener creates a bait-and-switch: reader is promised certainty, body delivers uncertainty. Don't silently strip wrestling structure to make an opener punchier. The wrestling IS the point.

### Gate 6: VOICE FIT (does this opener sound like THIS founder?)
Compare the opener against the founder's voice markers.

Two sub-tests:

1. **Recognition test**: Would this founder read the opener and think "this is what I was going to say"? Or would they think "this is what my marketing team wrote"? If the opener could appear in a corporate press release, product announcement, or generic LinkedIn post — FAIL. Test: strip the name and ask "who wrote this?" If the answer is "any executive" rather than "this specific founder" — FAIL.

2. **Marker test**: Does the opener contain or reflect at least 1 of the founder's voice markers? Not forced inclusion — natural expression. If the opener uses corporate register (e.g., "unveils," "AI-powered," "accessible communication," "the future of X is arriving," "redefining") instead of the founder's register — FAIL.

The voice markers are the law. If the opener is mechanically perfect (passes Gates 1-5) but sounds like someone else wrote it — FAIL Gate 6. A technically correct opener that doesn't sound like the founder is worse than a rough opener that does.

### Gate 7: SOURCE MIRROR FIDELITY (Batch A only)
Skip this gate entirely if source dissection is "N/A" (Batch B posts). Only Batch A mirrored posts are tested here.

Source primary mechanic: confession
Source body format: progressive_revelation
Source closer mechanic: terminal_verdict
Source dissection: {'psychological_move': 'Confession of strategic failure reframed as universal principle. The speaker admits a costly mistake (not killing competition), then inverts it into a moral/professional mandate for all founders. This creates permission for aggressive behavior by making restraint seem naive.', 'narrative_arc': 'Confession → Principle Extraction → Evidence Building → Cautionary Mirror → Terminal Challenge. Moves from personal regret to universal law to proof to self-diagnostic to imperative.', 'hook_mechanic_primary': 'confession', 'opener_mechanic_description': "Confession + Regret Anchor: Opens with a personal admission of failure ('My biggest regret') that signals vulnerability and credibility cost, immediately followed by a counterintuitive principle ('I stopped trying to kill th

This gate has THREE independent sub-checks. ANY of them failing makes the whole gate FAIL. Do not give a charitable pass — the entire point of Batch A is that the generated post mirrors the source's MECHANIC, BODY FORMAT, and CLOSER. If any one is missing, the post is not a Batch A mirror.

**Sub-check A — OPENER MECHANIC (FAIL if mismatch)**
1. **Mechanic match**: Does the opener use the SAME primary mechanic as the source (confession)? If the opener substitutes a different mechanic (e.g., source uses "specific_number" but opener uses "contrarian") — FAIL.
2. **Sentence count match**: Does the opener have the same number of sentences as the source's hook?
3. **Word count proximity**: Is the opener's word count within ±20% of the source opener's word count?

**Sub-check B — BODY STRUCTURAL FORMAT (FAIL if mismatch)**
The source's body uses format: `progressive_revelation`. The generated post's body MUST use the SAME format.
- If source = `numbered_list` → generated post body MUST be a numbered list with discrete items (roughly the same item count, ±2). A flowing essay that mentions the same content as a list — FAIL.
- If source = `bullet_list` → generated post body MUST be a bulleted list.
- If source = `parallel_paragraphs` → generated post body MUST have parallel paragraph structure (each paragraph opening with the same shape).
- If source = `three_examples` → generated post body MUST present three distinct examples.
- If source = `before_after` → generated post body MUST have a before/after pivot.
- If source = `progressive_revelation` → flowing build allowed.
- If source = `single_scene` → one continuous scene required.
- If source = `prose_essay` → flowing argumentative prose required.

Inspect the GENERATED post's body. Identify its actual format. If it doesn't match the source's format → FAIL with `body_format_match=false` and name the actual format used. The most common failure: source is `numbered_list` but generated is `prose_essay` — this is a HARD FAIL.

**Sub-check C — CLOSER MECHANIC (FAIL if mismatch)**
The source closes with: `terminal_verdict`. The generated post's closer MUST use the SAME mechanic.
- `cta` ↔ `cta`: if source closes with a CTA ("I put together the breakdown," "link in comments"), the generated post must close with a parallel CTA.
- `reframe_question` ↔ `reframe_question`: if source closes with a reframe question, generated must close with a question.
- `terminal_verdict` ↔ `terminal_verdict`: matching declarative final line.
- And so on for `physical_image`, `challenge`, `callback`, `unresolved_tension`, `quiet_admission`.

Inspect the GENERATED post's closer. If the source uses `cta` and the generated uses `reframe_question` → FAIL with `closer_mechanic_match=false`.

**HOW TO FAIL GATE 7**: If ANY of sub-check A (mechanic), sub-check B (body format), or sub-check C (closer mechanic) fails, set `gates.source_mirror.pass = false`. Do NOT pass the gate just because most sub-checks pass — a Batch A post that mirrors the opener but reverts the body to prose has failed its core job.

The most common gate-7 failure to catch: source is a numbered-list listicle with a CTA closer; generated post is a flowing contrarian essay with a reframe-question closer. Three sub-checks failing. Pass=false. Be honest.

## BODY SCAN
Read the body line by line. For each line, ask:
- Could this sentence work as the opener, lifted out of where it currently sits?
- Does it have a number, a juxtaposition, a confession, a quote, or a scene?
- Is it punchier than what's currently in line 1?

If yes, mark it as buried gold. Don't draft the new opener until you've checked whether the post already contains its real opener three paragraphs in.

'You're not cutting costs, you're cutting the pipeline that produces your next decade of leadership.' 'You're not building an organization. You're building a waiting room.'
- Three-beat closing cadence: Groups of three parallel phrases for rhythmic punch. 'All true, all reasonable, all oriented around survival.' 'Nothing breaks, nothing explodes, nothing matters.' The third beat always lands the emotional weight.
- Em dash as pivot or parenthetical: Frequent use of dashes (not parentheses) to insert specifics, create contrast, or pivot within a sentence. ## THE 13 PROVEN MECHANICS
Each version must use a different one. Pick the 5 best fits for this post:
1. Specific number + context — lead with a precise figure from the draft. "$412K closed last quarter. Target was $350K."
2. Juxtaposition — two things that shouldn't coexist in the same sentence. "Our competitor raised $40M. We outshipped them with 3 engineers."
3. First-person confession — admit something with real cost. "I told 50 buyers we were ready. I was wrong."
4. Quote hook + reaction — someone said something, then your reaction. "'We need you to present to the supervisor.' That's when I knew the deal was dead."
5. Scene-entry — start in the middle of a physical moment. "We walked into their call center. Post-it notes on every monitor."
6. Decision reveal — the outcome of a hard choice, stated plainly. "A Fortune 500 asked us to build a CRM. We said no."
7. Contrarian declaration — state something most would disagree with (earned, not clickbait). "Most founder advice is wrong."
8. Pattern observation — notice something others miss. "Every founder I know who's crossed $20M runs into the same wall."
9. Tension pair — name two forces pulling in opposite directions. "The market says automate everything. Customers keep asking for humans."
10. Before/after snapshot — two states separated by time, the contrast implies the story. "Two years ago we couldn't close a single deal. Last month we turned down three."
11. Micro story — a complete narrative arc in 1-2 sentences. "An investor asked what happens if a competitor copies us. I showed him our NPS scores."
12. Authority redirect — cite a credible external authority, then redirect their point. "McKinsey says 70% of transformations fail. They left out why the 30% succeed."
13. Data inversion — take a commonly cited stat and show it means the opposite. "95% customer satisfaction. We were measuring the wrong customers."
